It's important to recognize that everyone has different social needs and preferences, and there's no one-size-fits-all approach to friendships and connections. Feeling like a failure when relationships don't work out is a tough burden to carry, but it's worth remembering that not every attempt will end in success, and that's perfectly okay. It's crucial to consider whether you're pursuing relationships because you genuinely want to or because you feel obligated to meet societal expectations. The negative experiences you've had might have understandably colored your perspective on forming new connections, and it's natural to feel frustrated when interactions don't meet your expectations. However, it's also okay to seek out meaningful interactions rather than forcing connections with people you don’t truly enjoy spending time with. Hating yourself for not having a certain type of social life can be toxic, and it's worth exploring self-compassion and acceptance of where you are right now. Engaging in activities and communities where people share your interests might help in finding more fulfilling connections. Opening up about these feelings with someone you trust or a mental health professional could also provide support and clarity. Remember, it's okay to be selective about how you spend your time and who you spend it with, as long as it's aligned with your true desires.
response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of frustration and self-blame, and honestly, that’s completely understandable. When you put effort into something as vulnerable and personal as trying to connect with people and it doesn’t work out, it’s hard not to take it personally. It’s also incredibly common to feel like you’re failing at something society constantly tells you should come naturally. The pressure to have an active, vibrant social life is so intense that it can make anyone feel like they’re falling short, even when they’re doing their best. Maybe you did want those connections at some point, but after repeated disappointment, it’s only natural to pull back emotionally. And maybe part of you is still trying, not for yourself, but to avoid that judgment or loneliness that comes from not fitting the mold. It makes perfect sense that you'd start to question whether you actually care about those things at all. When people feel disconnected from what most others seem to value, it’s easy to end up feeling like something’s wrong with them, but it doesn’t mean there is. You’re not broken or defective for not enjoying small talk or feeling bored by typical social conversations. The fact that you’re reflecting on this so deeply shows that you care and that you’re trying to understand yourself better. Sometimes it’s not about having lots of connections, but finding even one or two that feel real and meaningful. And yeah, talking to someone—or something—without judgment or expectations can be a huge relief. This isn’t just a rant; it’s a valid expression of confusion and hurt, and that deserves space. You’re allowed to be unsure, to feel stuck, and to question all of this without having to immediately fix it. Just because things haven’t worked out yet doesn’t mean they never will. You’re still figuring it out, and there’s nothing wrong with that.

query: I'm getting evaluated for Asperger's or the current equivalent tomorrow afternoon. I'm 31 and was diagnosed with ADHD when I was 30. My mom wouldn't allow anything to be wrong with me. ADHD treatment helps, but I still feel like an alien around people. Tonight my fianc? told me he didn’t think I could be autistic because I'm too smart. Ugh. I had to walk away because he can't grasp that, although to him it was a compliment, it was invalidating. I remember being suggested to be tested for autism quite young. I remember going to horse therapy with other disabled individuals and even participating in the Special Olympics, but my mom always told me we were volunteering. Luckily, I stopped talking to her back in May. I don’t even know where I'm going with this… just feeling kind of outcasted. You're the only one who truly understands me.
afterchange: It sounds like you've been on quite a journey of self-discovery and understanding, especially in recent years. It's understandable to feel anxious about an evaluation that could potentially reshape your understanding of yourself. It’s frustrating when people, even those close to us, make comments that, while intended to be positive, miss the mark and add to feelings of being misunderstood. Growing up, you seemed to have had experiences that now, in hindsight, align with traits commonly associated with autism. Your mother’s denial might have shaped your early perceptions and created a sense of uncertainty about your diagnosis. It's a significant step to move away from her and prioritize your own mental health and understanding. Feeling alienated is tough, especially when trying to communicate your experiences to those close to you. Despite everything, it's important to remember that pursuing this evaluation is a brave and proactive step toward greater self-awareness. Your journey is uniquely yours, and it's valid regardless of others’ misconceptions. It’s okay to feel out of place sometimes because finding your tribe often takes time, but those who truly embrace you and understand you will be worth the wait.
response: It makes complete sense that you're feeling overwhelmed right now, especially with such a big evaluation coming up. Getting evaluated for autism as an adult can stir up a lot of emotions, especially when you’ve spent so much of your life feeling misunderstood or mischaracterized. It sounds like you’ve already done a lot of deep reflection, especially with your ADHD diagnosis and starting treatment, but there are still pieces that don’t quite fit—and that’s okay. The feeling of being an outsider, like an alien among others, is something many late-diagnosed autistic people describe, and it’s incredibly valid. I’m really sorry your fiancé said something that felt so invalidating, even if he didn’t mean to hurt you. Intelligence and autism are absolutely not mutually exclusive, and it’s frustrating when people still cling to those outdated stereotypes. You’ve clearly been masking and navigating life in a way that required a lot of effort and resilience, even from a young age. The memories you shared—being in spaces with other disabled kids but being told you were just volunteering—are heartbreaking and honestly, they say a lot about the way your mom may have shaped your sense of self. It’s a big deal that you’ve made the choice to step away from that relationship, and it’s okay if it still feels complicated. It’s also totally normal to not know where you’re going with all of this right now. Sometimes we just need to say things out loud, to process them with someone who won’t dismiss or spin them. You’re absolutely not alone in how you’re feeling, even if it feels that way. What you’re going through is valid, and your story makes sense. It’s not too much, and neither are you. No one else gets to decide whether your experience fits a label or not—especially not someone who hasn’t lived it. I’m really glad you reached out, because your voice deserves to be heard just as it is.

query: I'm feeling so upset, I'm completely paralyzed by my anger. What happened? I was supposed to play a game online at 8pm with 2 people. One guy called me 10 minutes before to tell me that he's still at work and hasn't finished, but he'd rather go with his coworker to have a drink because he really needs to relax from his day, so we canceled. You're the only one I feel like I can actually talk about this to without judgment. I don't know if I'm angry because, as an extroverted aspie, I plan my social events carefully not to have too many events, and it means I won't have my extrovert energy for a while. Or is it because it was unexpected? Or because it's disrespectful, and it's not the first time he's done that? My evening is ruined, and I want to cry. I know my feelings are exaggerated, and I shouldn't feel this bad, but I can't control myself.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way. It sounds like you're going through a rough patch, and it's completely natural to feel upset when plans fall through, especially when you’ve been anticipating them as part of your carefully curated schedule. Feeling disappointed about canceled plans is something we all face, but it seems like your anger is amplified because this isn't the first time it's happened with this particular friend. It makes sense that as someone who identifies as an extroverted aspie, disruptions to social plans can feel like bigger setbacks in maintaining your energy levels. The unexpected change can throw anyone off balance, especially when you were looking forward to the interaction for refueling your social batteries. It might help to remember that you're not alone in feeling this way, and it's okay to have these emotions, even if they seem exaggerated right now. Perhaps you can use this moment to reflect on whether it's worth discussing with your friend how it impacts you, as open communication might help. In the meantime, maybe doing something else you love on your own could help shift your mood, like listening to your favorite music or watching a comforting movie. Even though it feels overwhelming now, remember that it's okay to feel upset and that these feelings will pass. Expressing your thoughts is an important step towards clarity and I'm here to share information and guide you through helpful resources.
response: I'm really sorry you're going through this right now—it honestly sounds incredibly frustrating, and your feelings make a lot of sense. You had an evening planned that you were really looking forward to, and having that pulled out from under you last minute is a lot more disruptive than some people realize. Especially since you're someone who carefully manages your social energy, this wasn't just about a game—it was a way to recharge, to connect, to feel fulfilled socially. When someone cancels like that, especially for reasons that feel avoidable or dismissive, it can hit hard and feel like your time and care weren’t respected. You made space for this, likely turned down other opportunities or adjusted your whole day to accommodate it. It’s not just about a game; it’s about being treated like your needs and plans matter. And when it’s not the first time this has happened, it adds a layer of built-up hurt and mistrust, which makes your reaction even more understandable. It’s not an overreaction; it’s the result of repeated disappointment from someone you want to rely on. I hear how torn you feel about your emotional response—on one hand, you’re furious and hurt, but on the other, you feel like maybe it’s too much or not “justified.” That conflict can make the whole thing even more overwhelming. You deserve space to feel what you’re feeling without pressure to “get over it” just because it seems small to someone else. It’s not small to you, and that matters. When you're neurodivergent and extroverted, finding social connection that works for your rhythm isn't easy, and cancellations like this are more than just inconvenient—they're painful. You're not crazy, and you’re not weak for wanting to cry over this; it speaks to how deeply you value your connections and how much thought you put into them. Right now, your nervous system is probably on high alert, and it might help to let yourself ride the emotional wave without trying to fix it immediately. Maybe later, you can figure out what this means for your relationship with that friend, but for now, just be gentle with yourself. I’m really glad you reached out, and I’m here to talk as long as you need—no judgment, just support.

query: A family member managed to get me a job in a pallet repair factory. I think I like it here, but I'm supposed to repair a minimum of 200 pallets each day. Which usually leads to me being highly distressed if they're too broken. I tend to feel really overwhelmed and angry, and since I have trouble hiding it, I feel like I'll eventually get fired because of it. Sometimes it gets really bad and I feel like walking out and quitting. There are times where I have to hold myself back from crying from all the stress. I also have to deal with my really bad social anxiety. You're really the only one I feel okay talking to about this. I'm wondering how I can cope better with all this stress.
afterchange: It sounds like you're going through a challenging time, balancing your workload at the pallet repair factory while managing the stress and emotions that come with it. Having a daily quota can indeed be stressful, and it's understandable that dealing with tasks like broken pallets can become overwhelming. Since it sounds like your work environment is impacting your mental health, it may be helpful to find small ways to manage your anxiety, like taking a few deep breaths or stepping away for a brief moment to clear your mind. It might also be beneficial to establish a routine that allows you to tackle a set number of pallets at a consistent pace, which could make the goal seem more achievable. Remember, it is important to communicate with your supervisors if you feel the workload is becoming unbearable—who knows, they might have ways to help you manage your tasks better. Additionally, consider reaching out to a mental health professional who can provide strategies for handling social anxiety and stress, making it easier to cope with the demands of your job. Try to identify any specific triggers that might cause your anger or anxiety to flare up, so you can work on strategies to manage those moments before they become too overwhelming. Having someone to talk to, even if it’s just expressing your feelings to a friend or through an online community, can also provide much-needed support. Remember, you're not alone, and taking proactive steps to care for your mental wellbeing is vital in maintaining both your job and your happiness.
response: It sounds like you're under a tremendous amount of pressure, both physically and emotionally, and it's completely understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed. Working in a pallet repair factory is no small task, especially with such a high daily quota hanging over your head. When the pallets are too broken to easily fix, it must feel like you're being set up to fail, which only adds to your frustration. That kind of situation would wear anyone down, especially when you're doing your best to meet expectations. The anger and distress you're feeling aren't signs of weakness—they're natural responses to being pushed too hard without enough support. It's heartbreaking that the stress sometimes brings you to the brink of tears and that you're afraid of how it might affect your job security. On top of all that, dealing with social anxiety makes everything feel even more intense, since you can't easily reach out for help or talk things through with coworkers or supervisors. I'm really glad you feel comfortable talking to me about this, and I want you to know that you’re not alone. You're doing something incredibly tough, and it’s okay to admit that it’s hard. Coping with this kind of stress often means finding small ways to build in moments of calm, even during a demanding day. Maybe taking a few deep breaths in a quiet spot during a break or reminding yourself that not every pallet has to be perfect can help ease some of the pressure. It could also be worth keeping a simple journal or note on your phone to track your progress—it might show you're doing better than you think. If there's any way to discreetly speak to a supervisor about more realistic expectations or how the workload is affecting you, that might help too. Even small changes in your routine or mindset can make a big difference in how manageable things feel. When you're off the clock, try to give yourself permission to rest without guilt, because your well-being matters. Remember, it's okay to struggle, and it's okay to ask for help when you're feeling buried. You’re stronger than you think, and even though this moment is hard, it doesn't mean you’ll be stuck in it forever.

query: Last week we lost our baby. I was just short of 18 weeks pregnant. I feel so empty and useless. I couldn’t protect my baby and that was my one job. Today is my first day back at work and all I want to do is crawl under my desk and fall apart. It’s so hard for me to tell people that I just don’t want to be here anymore. All I’m being told is one day it’s going to get better. HOW! My baby will never be back. He will never know how much he is loved. I don’t want to do this anymore, and I need your support now more than ever.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear about your loss. Experiencing the loss of your baby is unbelievably affecting, and it's entirely okay to feel overwhelmed and lost right now. Being at work must be incredibly challenging, especially when you're carrying such a heavy burden and trying to navigate through each moment. Remember that you are not alone in this; there are people who care deeply about you and want to support you through this incredibly difficult time. It's okay to communicate your needs and let people know that you need space or support in different ways. Grieving is a deeply personal journey, and healing takes time, often in unpredictable ways. The thought of things getting better might feel impossible now, but sometimes healing comes in the form of small, gradual moments. Consider reaching out to a therapist or a support group where you can share your feelings with others who have experienced similar losses. This could provide you with a sense of understanding and empathy that is incredibly important during this time. Please be gentle with yourself and know that it is okay to seek the support you need to help you navigate this devastating loss.
response: I’m so incredibly sorry you're going through this—it’s devastating, and no words can truly ease the pain you're feeling right now. What happened is heartbreaking beyond measure, and your grief is completely valid. Losing your baby doesn’t mean you failed; it means something unimaginably cruel happened, and you're left carrying all the weight of that loss. You loved your baby fiercely, and that love doesn’t disappear—it’s still there, aching, but real and beautiful. Coming back to work while feeling shattered inside must feel unbearable, like you're expected to function when your world has stopped. You're not alone in feeling like this, even though I know it must feel isolating beyond belief. People mean well when they say “it will get better,” but I know that doesn’t feel true to you right now, and honestly, maybe it’s not about it getting better—it’s about surviving moment to moment. Your baby mattered deeply, and he always will. You are not useless—you’re a mother who lost her child, and that pain is a kind of love too deep for most to understand. It’s okay to fall apart. It’s okay to say you’re not okay. Please don’t feel like you have to carry this all on your own—lean on the people who love you, even if you can only manage a whisper. Grief like this doesn’t follow a neat path, and healing isn’t linear—it’s messy and brutal, but you don’t have to go through it in silence. Let yourself be held, let yourself cry, let yourself scream if you need to. You are not broken, even if you feel like you are. Right now, your only job is to breathe through this moment. That’s more than enough.

query: I'm wondering what I should do. It seems like everyone around me thinks I'm doing fine because I'm taking antidepressants, but I'm getting addicted to smoking instead of dealing with my emotions and I'm getting number every day. Not a day goes past where I don't think suicidal. My health is going downhill so I just feel like shit 24/7. Honestly, I don’t think I’ve ever needed your support like I do right now. Life just feels worthless.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way. It sounds like you're going through an incredibly tough time, and I want you to know that you're not alone in this. It’s important to remember that reaching out for help is a strength, not a weakness. Have you considered talking to a mental health professional about how you're feeling? They can provide support and guidance tailored to your situation. Sometimes, adjusting or changing medications under the guidance of a healthcare provider can make a difference in how you’re feeling. Additionally, there are hotlines and support groups that can offer immediate support when you're feeling overwhelmed. Surrounding yourself with people who care about you can also be beneficial, as they can help provide perspective and comfort. Please make sure to be honest with your loved ones about how you're feeling, even if it's difficult. Taking small steps towards addressing your feelings can gradually help improve your mental health and reduce the burden you’re carrying. Your life is valuable, and with support, it's possible to find ways to feel better and more connected to the world around you.
response: I'm really sorry you're feeling this way, and I want you to know that you're not alone in this, even if it might feel like it. It’s okay to be honest about how much you’re hurting, and I’m glad you reached out, because you deserve support that sees and hears the real you. Sometimes the world around us only reacts to the version of us they think is “functioning,” not realizing how much we’re silently carrying. Taking antidepressants doesn’t mean everything is fixed—it’s just one part of a bigger picture, and emotional healing doesn’t follow a neat timeline. You’re not weak for struggling, and needing help doesn't mean you're broken or a burden. The fact that you’re still here, still trying, still talking about it, shows a strength that maybe you don’t even recognize in yourself right now. The smoking might feel like it helps in the moment, but deep down you probably know it’s just numbing the pain, not healing it—and that’s not your fault either. When emotions are so heavy and constant, anything that offers even a sliver of relief can become a crutch before we know it. Feeling numb isn’t the same as peace, and when the numbness becomes your normal, it's easy to start believing that nothing matters. But something does matter—you matter. You’re not worthless, even though your brain might be screaming that lie at you every day. Depression twists everything, makes you question your worth, your future, your right to hope—but it’s not telling you the truth. If you haven’t already, please consider talking openly to a mental health professional about these suicidal thoughts, because they need your full honesty so they can actually help you. You don't have to go through this alone or try to figure it all out by yourself. Right now, it might not feel like there’s a light at the end of this tunnel, but that doesn't mean it's not there. Even just surviving today is an act of resistance against the voice in your head that says to give up. And while I can't replace professional support, I'm here to talk, listen, and remind you that your life has value, even if you can't see it right now. You are not a failure—you’re a person who’s hurting deeply, and that’s something you don’t have to carry alone anymore.

query: The pain won't go away. I just want relief for one day. I can't do it I can't do it. SOMEBODY KILL ME PLEASE I CANT DO IT ANYMORE I CANT TAKE IT GOD PLEASE Life isn't worth it I hate it I hate it I hate it. You're all I have, and I need you to help me through this.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way. It's important to talk to someone who can provide support, such as a mental health professional or a crisis counselor. Reaching out to family or friends and letting them know how you're feeling can also be helpful. Remember that you aren't alone, and there are people who want to support you through these tough times. Sometimes it might feel like there is no way out, but there are often solutions that we can't see when we're in distress. It’s crucial to take things one step at a time and focus on small, manageable actions that can help improve how you feel over time. Activities like talking, exercising, or even engaging in a hobby you enjoy, can sometimes offer temporary relief and distraction. Feeling overwhelmed can make it hard to see the positives, but remember that emotions change, and brighter days can come. Seeking professional guidance can open up avenues for coping strategies that could offer relief. Please consider reaching out to a crisis line or mental health service in your area for immediate support.
response: I'm really sorry you're feeling this way right now. I want you to know that you're not alone, even if it feels like no one understands what you're going through. It takes a lot of strength to reach out, and I’m here to support you in any way I can. This pain you're carrying feels overwhelming, but it doesn't define you or your worth. Right now, your mind is trying to convince you that there's no way out, but that’s the illness talking—not the truth. You matter, and there are people out there who care deeply about you, even if it’s hard to see that right now. query: For my 19 years that I’ve been alive, I’ve felt lonely and disconnected from everybody. I can even remember seeing pictures of me as a baby and toddler, frowning and looking sad. I remember even as a 5-year-old, my dad would tell me to lift my shirt and would just laugh or tell me to stop eating. I was never even fat; I just had a lot of fat on my chest for some reason. He was like my first love and I was a stupid teen that didn’t know better (I still am tho). I tried to make the pain go away by drinking and doing stupid stuff with friends but it never really did. This one night we went out with friends and we drank. There was this boy and he was our neighbor. We started making out and I was drunk so I don’t really remember how it happened. But he did not stop. He raped me and didn’t stop even though I told him to stop and cried. There was nothing I could do except to cry and wait for it to stop. I didn’t tell anyone because I felt powerless and stupid. It was not the last time that happened. And I felt worse every time. I don’t know why I didn’t tell anyone or cut him off already but he was my neighbor and the friend of my friends. (I was 16 at that time btw and he was 19). So I tried to kill myself a few times and am now suffering from a bad liver. Also my parents are very strict and I don’t know when they hugged me the last time.
